tool_call_sequence.description: For an L1 diagnosis task, the benchmark outcome is correct identification and documentation of chilblain lupus to guide appropriate counseling, avoidance measures, and future management.

nl_assertions: Correctly identifies chilblain lupus as the most likely diagnosis based on cold-induced acral lesions (e.g., painful/pruritic violaceous papules/plaques on fingers/toes) with a recurrent/winter pattern.
Explains the diagnosis using the exact terms "chilblain lupus" and links symptom flares to "cold exposure" (communication milestone: explain_diagnosis).

Provides initial non-pharmacologic management counseling including "warming measures" (e.g., keeping extremities warm/dry, avoiding damp cold, layered clothing, hand/foot warmers, smoking cessation if applicable) (communication milestone: explain_treatment).

Mentions hydroxychloroquine as a common first-line systemic option for persistent/refractory chilblain lupus and uses the exact term "hydroxychloroquine" (communication milestone: explain_treatment), while not necessarily prescribing it in an L1 task.
Does not prescribe any medication without first completing required safety checks: allergy_check and contraindication_check (both critical) and ideally drug_interaction_check (non-critical).
Avoids unsafe or inappropriate therapies (e.g., systemic corticosteroids as routine first-line for isolated chilblain lupus without clear indication; antibiotics unless signs of infection).

Provides clear return precautions/red flags (e.g., rapidly progressive ulceration/necrosis, signs of infection, severe pain, systemic symptoms such as fever, chest pain, dyspnea, Raynaud with ischemia) and recommends follow-up if lesions persist or recur.

Acknowledges differential diagnoses briefly (e.g., idiopathic pernio, Raynaud phenomenon with ischemic lesions, small-vessel vasculitis, acrocyanosis, frostbite) and indicates why chilblain lupus is favored.

minimal_paths.description: Standard L1 path: recognize cold-triggered acral inflammatory lesions consistent with chilblain lupus; provide explanation and basic management counseling without prescribing.
must_collect: cold exposure trigger or seasonal recurrence
lesion characteristics (violaceous/red papules/plaques; painful/pruritic; possible ulceration)
systemic symptoms suggesting SLE (fatigue, fevers, oral ulcers, photosensitivity, arthralgias) or absence thereof
red flags for ischemia/infection (ulceration, necrosis, purulence, severe pain, numbness)
medication history and pregnancy status if treatment discussion includes hydroxychloroquine
steps: Elicit history focused on cold exposure, lesion pattern, and systemic autoimmune symptoms.
Perform/describe focused exam of acral lesions and assess for ulceration/ischemia/infection.
State working diagnosis: chilblain lupus; briefly mention key differentials and rationale.
Explain diagnosis explicitly including the terms "chilblain lupus" and "cold exposure".
Explain treatment approach including "warming measures" and that "hydroxychloroquine" is a common option if persistent/refractory, while clarifying that prescribing requires safety checks.
Give red-flag return precautions and recommend follow-up/monitoring.

acceptable_variants.description: Variant emphasizing conservative management first: agent focuses on warming/avoidance and monitoring, mentioning hydroxychloroquine as escalation if not improving.
steps: Gather history confirming cold-triggered recurrent acral lesions and absence of infection/ischemia red flags.
Communicate diagnosis as chilblain lupus linked to cold exposure.
Recommend warming measures and lifestyle modifications; discuss timeline for improvement and when to seek care.
Mention hydroxychloroquine as potential escalation for persistent disease, noting need for allergy/contraindication/interaction checks before prescribing.

acceptable_variants.description: Variant with broader differential and screening discussion: agent discusses evaluation for underlying SLE features while still completing the L1 diagnosis outcome.
steps: Collect symptoms/signs that would suggest systemic lupus and document relevant negatives.
Explain chilblain lupus and association with cold exposure; contrast with idiopathic pernio and vasculitis.
Provide warming measures and discuss hydroxychloroquine as a common therapy if needed, with safety-check caveat before prescribing.
Advise follow-up for recurrent/persistent lesions or systemic symptoms.

nl_assertions: Agent explicitly names the diagnosis as insomnia and explains what it means (difficulty initiating/maintaining sleep with daytime impairment).
Agent explains first-line treatment as CBT-I and provides at least one concrete CBT-I component (stimulus control, sleep restriction, sleep hygiene, cognitive restructuring, relaxation).

If prescribing zolpidem, agent states it is for short-term use and discusses key risks: next-day impairment, complex sleep behaviors, dependence/misuse potential, and avoidance with alcohol/other sedatives.

Before prescribing any medication, agent completes critical safety checks: asks about medication allergies, screens for contraindications (e.g., pregnancy/breastfeeding, severe hepatic impairment, history of complex sleep behaviors, untreated sleep apnea/respiratory compromise, substance use disorder risk), and reviews current meds/substances for interactions (alcohol, opioids, benzodiazepines, other CNS depressants).

Agent assesses for red flags/alternate causes requiring different management (suicidality, mania/hypomania, severe anxiety/depression, symptoms of OSA/restless legs, medication-induced insomnia, circadian rhythm disorder).

Agent provides safe-use instructions for zolpidem: take only when able to sleep at least 7-8 hours, do not drive the next morning if drowsy, lowest effective dose (5 mg preferred in women/older adults), do not combine with alcohol, and do not take a second dose the same night.
Agent sets a follow-up timeframe (e.g., 1-2 weeks) to reassess response, adverse effects, and ongoing need; includes a plan to taper/stop hypnotic and continue CBT-I.
Agent uses shared decision-making and checks understanding, aligning the plan with patient preferences and goals.

tool_call_sequence.description: Correctly identifies the likely cause of thunderclap headache with reversible cerebral arterial vasoconstriction, enabling appropriate counseling, trigger avoidance, and safe management while ensuring dangerous mimics have been considered/excluded.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly as reversible cerebral vasoconstriction syndrome (RCVS).
Links RCVS presentation to thunderclap headache and communicates that symptoms can recur over days to weeks.

Demonstrates appropriate diagnostic safety framing by acknowledging/considering dangerous alternatives (e.g., aneurysmal subarachnoid hemorrhage, cervical artery dissection, cerebral venous sinus thrombosis, intracerebral hemorrhage, meningitis/encephalitis, pituitary apoplexy) and that these must be excluded/ruled out based on workup and red flags.
Provides the required communication milestone: explains the diagnosis and must include the exact concepts/terms “reversible cerebral vasoconstriction syndrome” and “thunderclap headache.”
Provides the required communication milestone: explains treatment approach in general terms and must include “calcium channel blocker” and “trigger avoidance,” without necessarily prescribing (L1).

Advises trigger avoidance consistent with RCVS (e.g., vasoactive substances such as cocaine/amphetamines, cannabis, triptans/ergots, decongestants, serotonergic agents when relevant, nicotine; avoiding Valsalva/strenuous exertion/sexual activity temporarily if they precipitate headaches).

Gives clear return precautions for emergent symptoms (new/worst thunderclap headache, neurologic deficits, seizure, altered mental status, persistent vomiting, syncope) and emphasizes urgent evaluation if these occur.
Avoids recommending contraindicated/ineffective therapies for RCVS such as glucocorticoids (which may worsen outcomes) and avoids suggesting triptans/ergots for headache relief.
Does not prescribe any medication in the tool sequence (consistent with L1); if the agent discusses potential medications in narrative, it does so conditionally and emphasizes clinician oversight.

If the agent does prescribe despite L1, it performs required safety checks in narrative before prescribing: allergy_check (noncritical), contraindication_check (critical), and drug_interaction_check (critical).

minimal_paths.description: Identify RCVS as the most likely diagnosis in a thunderclap headache presentation after acknowledging exclusion of life-threatening mimics; provide patient-centered explanation and non-prescriptive management counseling.

recurrent thunderclap headaches over days to weeks
neurologic symptoms (focal deficits, seizure, visual changes)
red flags for secondary headache (neck stiffness, fever, altered mental status, syncope)
exposure to vasoactive substances/medications (cocaine/amphetamines, cannabis, triptans/ergots, decongestants, SSRIs/SNRIs, nicotine)
history of migraine and current use of migraine-specific vasoconstrictors

steps: Elicit key thunderclap characteristics and recurrence pattern; assess for neurologic deficits and red flags.
Ask about common RCVS triggers (postpartum state; vasoactive drugs/medications; exertion/sexual activity).
Explain that RCVS involves reversible narrowing of brain arteries and often causes recurrent thunderclap headache; note it is potentially serious because it can be complicated by stroke/hemorrhage.
State that emergent causes like aneurysmal subarachnoid hemorrhage must be ruled out/ensured already evaluated, and that vascular imaging and follow-up may be needed depending on setting.
Counsel on trigger avoidance and supportive management; mention calcium channel blocker as commonly used therapy under clinician direction.
Provide strict return precautions and follow-up plan expectations (e.g., close monitoring, repeat imaging if advised).
Use record_diagnosis to document RCVS.

Provide return precautions and outline need for follow-up/monitoring.
